questionText: Four treasure chests have number locks: 21, 23, 25, and 27. Consider this claim: "Because all four numbers are odd, exactly three of them are prime." Which option best evaluates the claim using factors?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can explain why a number is prime or composite
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Factors, Multiples & Primes: Prime & Composite Numbers, aligned to Students can explain why a number is prime or composite.
options: [{"text":"The claim is correct because every odd number is prime except multiples of 10.","correct":false},{"text":"The claim is correct because 21, 23, and 27 are prime, but 25 is composite since it is a square number.","correct":false},{"text":"The claim is incorrect because only 23 is prime; 21 = 3 × 7, 25 = 5 × 5, and 27 = 3 × 9 are composite.","correct":true},{"text":"The claim is incorrect because 23 and 25 are prime, while 21 and 27 are composite.","correct":false}]
explanation: The claim is incorrect. A prime number has exactly 2 factors: 1 and itself. 23 is prime because it cannot be made by multiplying two smaller whole numbers. But 21 = 3 × 7, 25 = 5 × 5, and 27 = 3 × 9, so each of those has factors other than 1 and itself and is composite. That means only 1 of the 4 odd numbers is prime, not 3. Option 1 is wrong because odd numbers are not always prime. Option 2 is wrong because 21 and 27 are also composite. Option 4 is wrong because 25 has factors 1, 5, and 25, so it is not prime.

questionText:
Consider these two claims about 17:
Claim 1: "17 is composite because it can be put into 1 group of 17."
Claim 2: "17 is prime because its only factor pair is 1 and 17."
Which evaluation is best?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can explain why a number is prime or composite
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Factors, Multiples & Primes: Prime & Composite Numbers, aligned to Students can explain why a number is prime or composite.
options: [{"text":"Claim 1 is correct, because making 1 group proves a number is composite.","correct":false},{"text":"Claim 2 is correct, because 17 has exactly two factors, 1 and 17, and Claim 1 would also be true for every whole number.","correct":true},{"text":"Both claims are correct, because 17 is odd and odd numbers are both prime and composite.","correct":false},{"text":"Neither claim is correct, because a prime number must have two different factor pairs.","correct":false}]
explanation: Claim 2 is correct. A prime number has exactly two factors, 1 and itself, and 17 has only 1 and 17. Claim 1 is not a valid test for being composite because every whole number can be put into 1 group of itself, including prime numbers. The idea that odd numbers are both prime and composite is false, and a prime number does not need two different factor pairs.

questionText:
Consider these ideas about the number 1:
Idea A: "1 is prime because 1 × 1 = 1."
Idea B: "1 is composite because every whole number can be multiplied."
Idea C: "1 is neither prime nor composite because it has only one factor."
Which evaluation is correct?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can explain why a number is prime or composite
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Factors, Multiples & Primes: Prime & Composite Numbers, aligned to Students can explain why a number is prime or composite.
options: [{"text":"Only Idea A is correct, because 1 × 1 shows two factors.","correct":false},{"text":"Only Idea B is correct, because all whole numbers are composite.","correct":false},{"text":"Only Idea C is correct, because 1 has one factor, while prime numbers have exactly two factors and composite numbers have more than two.","correct":true},{"text":"Ideas A and C are both correct, because 1 is a special kind of prime.","correct":false}]
explanation: Only Idea C is correct. The number 1 has just one factor: 1. A prime number must have exactly two factors, and a composite number must have more than two factors, so 1 is neither prime nor composite. Idea A is wrong because 1 and 1 are not two different factors; they are the same factor repeated. Idea B is wrong because not all whole numbers are composite.

questionText: A bakery packs cupcakes in equal rows. The total number of cupcakes is less than 20, is composite, and can be arranged in one factor pair with two different even numbers. Which total must it be?
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can explain why a number is prime or composite
summary: MCQ for Grade 4 Factors, Multiples & Primes: Prime & Composite Numbers, aligned to Students can explain why a number is prime or composite.
options: [{"text":"13, because 13 can be arranged as 1 × 13.","correct":false},{"text":"15, because 15 can be arranged as 3 × 5.","correct":false},{"text":"16, because 16 can be arranged as 2 × 8, and 2 and 8 are different even numbers.","correct":true},{"text":"19, because 19 can be arranged as 1 × 19.","correct":false}]
explanation: 16 is correct. It is less than 20, it is composite, and it has the factor pair 2 × 8, where both factors are even and different. 13 and 19 are prime because they only have the factor pairs 1 × 13 and 1 × 19. The number 15 is composite, but its factor pair 3 × 5 uses odd numbers, not two different even numbers.
